result: On quiet mornings I like to watch the city wake before the trains begin to rattle. From my kitchen window the street looks like a stage waiting for its actors. A delivery cyclist glides by, the wheels whispering on wet pavement, and a cat sits on the curb as if it owns the block. I brew coffee slowly, letting the aroma fill the rooms, and I read pages of whatever book happens to be near. Today it is a collection of sea stories. I imagine myself on a deck, the air salted, gulls calling through mist. The contrast between my still apartment and those imagined waves makes the moment feel balanced. Later the city will swell with motion, phones ringing, buses groaning, and the soft square of my window will frame a busy river of people. Yet this early hush belongs to me. It is a private corner of time, where thoughts can wander without collision. I think about small goals, like tending to the tomato plants on the balcony, or writing a letter to an old friend. I think about wider questions too: what makes a day good, how we measure ordinary joy. For now the kettle clicks, steam rises, and the first light touches the rooftops. I inhale, smile, and let the morning begin. Maybe I will take a walk to the river later, listen to ducks argue, and carry a notebook, because the act of noticing feels like an art worth practicing even when nothing seems special.
